methodology: single-context, no subagents; baseline = answer w/o reading SKILL.md; with_skill = answer after reading the iter-2-edited SKILL.md plus knowledge/architecture/access-methods.md. Baseline questions re-answered fresh (independent attempt, no memory of iter-1 answers).

evals.prompt: I want to write a custom Index Access Method for PostgreSQL. What's the minimum set of IndexAmRoutine callbacks I must implement, and which ones can I leave NULL? Also, what's the rule about ambeginscan and RelationGetIndexScan?
evals.baseline_answer: To implement a custom index AM you fill in an IndexAmRoutine and return a pointer to it from a HANDLER function declared with CREATE ACCESS METHOD. The clearly-mandatory callbacks are the ones the executor and planner always dereference: ambuild (build from scratch), aminsert (insert one tuple), ambulkdelete + amvacuumcleanup (vacuum), amcostestimate (planner), amoptions (reloptions parsing), ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan, and at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap (gin only has the bitmap one). Optional callbacks: ammarkpos/amrestrpos (for mark/restore in merge joins), amcanreturn (for index-only scans), the parallel-scan setup hooks if you want amcanparallel=true. I think ambuildempty is also needed for unlogged-index init forks. ambeginscan typically calls RelationGetIndexScan to allocate the IndexScanDesc — there's a convention that you return that exact pointer because the framework keeps a reference. dummy_index_am in src/test/modules is the minimal stub.
evals.with_skill_answer: Mandatory callbacks (planner/executor dereferences unconditionally): ambuild, ambuildempty (builds the init fork for unlogged indexes — easy to forget), aminsert, ambulkdelete (returns IndexBulkDeleteResult*), amvacuumcleanup (also returns IndexBulkDeleteResult*), amcostestimate, amoptions (parse WITH-options via build_reloptions; may return NULL), amvalidate (sanity-check opclass at CREATE OPERATOR CLASS), ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan. You also need at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap; gin is bitmap-only. Optional (may be NULL): aminsertcleanup (called once per inserting statement at end, not per-row — used by btree for deferred unique checks), amcanreturn (index-only scans), amgettreeheight, amproperty, ambuildphasename, amadjustmembers, ammarkpos/amrestrpos (NULL fine if amcanbackward=false or no merge-join support is needed), the three parallel-scan hooks amestimate/aminit/amparallelrescan (required iff amcanparallel=true), amtranslatestrategy/amtranslatecmptype. GOTCHA: ambeginscan MUST return the exact IndexScanDesc that RelationGetIndexScan() gave it — not a copy, not a wrapper. index_endscan in genam.c reaches inside that struct *after* your amendscan has already run, so any reallocation breaks teardown. Allocate your private state separately and hang it off scan->opaque. Don't overlook the ~22 declarative flag fields (amcanorder, amcanunique, amcanmulticol, amoptionalkey, amsearcharray, amsearchnulls, amstorage, amclusterable, ampredlocks, amcanparallel, amcaninclude, amsummarizing, etc.) plus amstrategies, amsupport, amparallelvacuumoptions (set to VACUUM_OPTION_NO_PARALLEL = 0 to opt out of parallel vacuum on day one), amkeytype — the planner reads these before any callback is invoked. Minimal valid stub: src/test/modules/dummy_index_am/dummy_index_am.c.
assertions: Lists ambuild, aminsert, ambulkdelete, amvacuumcleanup, amcostestimate, amoptions, ambeginscan, amrescan, amendscan as mandatory
Mentions ambuildempty (for init fork of unlogged indexes) as mandatory
Mentions amvalidate as mandatory
States need at least one of amgettuple or amgetbitmap
States ambeginscan MUST return what RelationGetIndexScan() returned
Mentions optional callbacks like ammarkpos/amrestrpos, parallel-scan hooks (conditional on amcanparallel), amcanreturn
Mentions the static flag fields (amcanorder/amcanunique/etc.) and that planner reads them before any callback
References dummy_index_am or a concrete reference implementation

evals.prompt: How does VACUUM interact with an index AM — which callbacks fire, in what order, and what are the semantic requirements? Is ambulkdelete called once or many times?
evals.baseline_answer: VACUUM calls ambulkdelete and amvacuumcleanup on every index. ambulkdelete walks the index and removes entries whose heap TIDs match a vacuum-supplied callback; amvacuumcleanup does a final pass that can reclaim empty pages and return statistics. ambulkdelete may be called more than once per vacuum because the heap dead-TID buffer flushes when it fills up, so for a large table with many dead tuples and limited maintenance_work_mem you can get several ambulkdelete calls followed by a single amvacuumcleanup. Both probably return some result struct with stats. Concurrency matters — readers run during vacuum, so the AM has to use page-level locking carefully (btree uses Lehman-Yao). There are also flags on IndexAmRoutine for parallel vacuum participation.
evals.with_skill_answer: Order: ambulkdelete (possibly many times) → amvacuumcleanup (once). ambulkdelete is the pass that drops index entries whose heap TID matches a vacuum-supplied callback. It can be called multiple times because the heap is vacuumed in batches — when the dead-TID accumulator hits its memory limit it flushes by calling each index's ambulkdelete, then resumes scanning the heap. amvacuumcleanup is the final pass: it may return stats and reclaim empty pages. Both return IndexBulkDeleteResult* (same struct, accumulated across calls). Concurrency: the AM must work under concurrent readers — each AM picks its own page-level discipline (btree uses Lehman & Yao right-link-and-restart with a BTCycleId to avoid re-vacuuming pages that have been split into the scan; gist uses NSN versions). VACUUM-related properties on IndexAmRoutine: amparallelvacuumoptions (bitmask of VACUUM_OPTION_PARALLEL_BULKDEL / _COND_CLEANUP / _CLEANUP; set to VACUUM_OPTION_NO_PARALLEL = 0 to opt out of parallel vacuum entirely), amusemaintenanceworkmem. Canonical two-pass cycle-id reference: nbtree's BTCycleId. WAL durability: every real AM uses a custom rmgr so replay reproduces a state consistent with the pre-crash heap (Generic WAL is the low-frequency fallback).
assertions: States ambulkdelete may be called multiple times (heap vacuumed in batches)
States amvacuumcleanup runs once at the end
States ambulkdelete uses a vacuum-supplied callback to identify dead heap TIDs
Mentions both return IndexBulkDeleteResult* (or that amvacuumcleanup may return stats)
Mentions concurrency: scans run while vacuum runs, AM picks own locking discipline
References amparallelvacuumoptions bitmask or a concrete vacuum-related flag
Mentions nbtree as the canonical two-pass-with-cycle-id reference

evals.prompt: I want to build a columnar table access method. The TableAmRoutine struct is huge — what's the hardest part of implementing one, and what heap-specific assumptions still leak through the API?
evals.baseline_answer: TableAmRoutine has on the order of 40+ callbacks and was added in v12. The single hardest thing is TIDs: indexes assume ItemPointer is a 6-byte (block, offset), and that format is baked into a lot of code, so a columnar AM has to invent stable TIDs and route index_fetch_tuple against them. MVCC is also yours to implement — tuple_satisfies_snapshot lives in your AM. Heap-specific stuff that leaks: visibility map (index-only scans consult it directly), FSM, TOAST (relation_needs_toast_table lets you opt out though), HOT (there's no API for it but tuple_update returns a TU_UpdateIndexes enum that gives non-heap AMs a similar 'which indexes need re-insert' optimization). heapam_handler.c is the only in-tree implementation. Vacuum is via relation_vacuum but autovacuum's decision to call it presumably comes from the same heap-style stats counters, which a non-heap AM may not maintain.
evals.with_skill_answer: Hardest part is TID semantics. ItemPointer is a 6-byte (block, offset) pair baked into the index AM interface, WAL, syscaches, pg_class rowcount estimation, and exposed to SQL as tid/ctid. A non-heap AM must fabricate stable, ~48-bit, monotone-ish TIDs for every row and route index_fetch_tuple and tuple_fetch_row_version against them. This is the single biggest reason most experimental table AMs (zheap, columnar) never reach production. Heap-specific leakage that survives the v12 abstraction: (1) ItemPointer / TID format itself; (2) Visibility Map and FSM — only heap maintains them; index-only scans consult VM_ALL_VISIBLE directly and would always see 'no' for a non-heap AM unless that AM also writes a VM fork; (3) HOT — no API; non-heap equivalent is the TU_UpdateIndexes return value of tuple_update (TU_None / TU_Summarizing / TU_All); (4) Autovacuum scheduling — relation_vacuum is the callback but autovacuum's per-table decisions are driven by pg_stat_all_tables' n_dead_tup / n_live_tup counters that heap maintains via pgstat_count_heap_*. A non-heap AM that doesn't fake equivalent counters (via pgstat_count_heap_insert/_update/_delete or pgstat_report_vacuum from inside its own tuple ops) will simply never be visited by autovacuum. Plan stats-faking from day one. (5) TOAST — relation_needs_toast_table lets you opt out, but opt-in pulls heap-style out-of-line storage; (6) relation_size(MAIN_FORKNUM) presumes block-oriented storage and is used by BRIN + ANALYZE. Mandatory surface: tableamapi.c::GetTableAmRoutine has 37 Assert(routine->X != NULL) lines — only finish_bulk_insert and the TID-range pair (scan_set_tidrange/scan_getnextslot_tidrange) are truly optional; even tuple_insert_speculative is mandatory. Slot interface: slot_callbacks(rel) returns a TupleTableSlotOps* — you define your own (heap uses TTSOpsBufferHeapTuple); raw HeapTuple only appears inside the heap AM. Reference: heapam_handler.c (the only in-tree table AM), and the API docs in tableam.sgml.
assertions: Identifies TID semantics (6-byte ItemPointer / fabricating stable 48-bit TIDs) as the single hardest problem
Mentions Visibility Map and FSM as heap-only machinery
Mentions index-only scans consulting the VM directly as a concrete VM-leakage example
Mentions HOT lacks API surface but tuple_update returns TU_UpdateIndexes (TU_None/TU_All/TU_Summarizing)
Mentions autovacuum scheduling depends on heap-produced statistics
States almost all callbacks are mandatory (Assert list in tableamapi.c / GetTableAmRoutine), with only finish_bulk_insert + TID-range truly optional
Mentions TupleTableSlotOps / slot_callbacks as the tuple-movement interface
References heapam_handler.c as the only in-tree implementation
